problem_summary: 2024 MCM/ICM Problem D (operations research / network flow): extend the IJC's "Plan 2014" to regulate water levels across the five Great Lakes by jointly scheduling the Compensating Works dam (Sault St. Marie, Lake Superior outflow) and the Moses-Saunders dam (Lake Ontario outflow into the St. Lawrence River). The goal is to optimize for the conflicting needs of seven stakeholder groups (domestic water supply, commercial navigation, hydropower, environment, recreational boaters, docking companies, riparian/shoreline owners) while hard-constraining against flooding and dangerously low water, motivated by the 2017 Lake Ontario flood that exposed Plan 2014's weaknesses. Team #2429211, Outstanding award.
models_used: Dynamic network flow model: Great Lakes abstracted to a 5-node DAG (Superior -> Huron/Michigan -> Erie -> Ontario -> St. Lawrence) with edge capacities c_i and transit/lag times t_i on controllable arcs

Ford-Fulkerson time-expansion: convert the dynamic (time-lagged) flow network into a static flow network by replicating each node per discretized timestep and wiring edges according to transit times, making it solvable as a standard flow/LP

Two-tier (multigranular) Linear Program: (1) Macroschedule LP over a multi-month horizon (daily timestep) that picks optimal achievable water heights via lake mass-balance constraints; (2) Microschedule LP over an intra-day/hourly horizon that times Moses-Saunders outflows, with a terminal constraint h_T = H-hat tying it back to the macro plan

L1 (absolute-deviation) objective: minimize stakeholder-weighted sum of |h_{i,t} - h_hat_{i,t}| plus a flow-tracking term |f_t - f_hat_t|; L1 keeps the program linear (abs values linearized via auxiliary variables)

Stakeholder utility / ideal-curve model: per-stakeholder target water-level curves built as sinusoidal oscillations about the historical mean (amplitude/frequency/offset tuned per group: e.g., environment wants annual oscillation for bird mating, riparian wants low+small+high-frequency for dune replenishment, navigation/hydropower/boaters want steady high levels, docks want low constant)

Adaptive fairness re-weighting heuristic: simulate ~5 months, commit 2 months, measure per-stakeholder net error eps_i = sum|h_t - h_hat_t|, then renormalize weights w_{1i} = eps_i / sum eps_k so the worst-served stakeholder is prioritized next horizon (a rolling-horizon / receding-horizon control loop)

Data-driven parameter estimation: time-lagged cross-correlation on water-level time series to recover inter-lake lag matrix (e.g., Superior->Michigan = 1 month, Superior->Ontario = 3 months); linear regression of distributary flow vs lake height (Niagara: f=2088.9*h_Erie-357993.8, R^2=0.928; Detroit: f=1611.6*h_Huron-278618.7, R^2=0.850), physically justified via linearized Bernoulli for small oscillations

Exogenous-inflow model: NOAA GLERL precipitation/runoff/evaporation data, cubic-spline interpolation of monthly totals to daily, with 25/50/75th-percentile scenarios; plus an ice-driven seasonal wave-attenuation factor and additive noise ('fuzzing') for robustness
key_techniques: Time-expanded graph trick to linearize a dynamic, lag-delayed flow problem into a static LP solvable by Simplex

Lake mass-balance as LP equality constraints: h_{i,t} = h_{i,t-1} + inflow - outflow, with surface-area division (flow/s_i) converting volumetric flow to marginal height and the index x_{t-L} encoding propagation lag
Linearizing |.| objective terms so the whole model stays an LP (fast, white-box, re-runnable)
Time-lagged cross-correlation to empirically infer physical transport delays instead of guessing them
Hard safety constraints (h <= F_i flood ceiling, h >= D_i drought floor) separating non-negotiable safety from soft stakeholder preferences in the objective
Receding-horizon control: simulate a buffer (3-month lag-aware lookahead) longer than the commit window to anticipate downstream effects before acting
Two-resolution decomposition (macro months / micro hours) coupled by a terminal-height handoff constraint
Scenario/percentile stress-testing (Q1/Q2/Q3 inflow) as the robustness experiment
Closing the loop with error-proportional weight renormalization for stakeholder equity
why_it_won: "It married a rigorous OR backbone (dynamic network flow + Ford-Fulkerson time-expansion + LP) to genuine domain grounding: every parameter (lag matrix, flow-height regressions, exogenous inflow) was estimated from the provided/NOAA data rather than assumed, and the physical justifications (linearized Bernoulli, ice wave attenuation) signal real understanding. The two-tier macro/micro decomposition directly answered the prompt's explicit ask about both long-horizon scheduling and proximal intra-day effects, with a clean terminal-constraint handoff. Crucially it delivered a non-obvious, defensible insight a judge can quote -- 'to manage Lake Ontario you must proactively manage Lake Superior 3 months ahead' -- backed by the Q1/Q2/Q3 robustness experiment showing Ontario stayed flat while Superior's schedule did the work. The human-centered framing (per-stakeholder sinusoidal ideal curves + an equity re-weighting heuristic) plus honest treatment of conflicting interests and controlled-flooding limitations reads as mature and self-aware, and the LP choice is explicitly defended on speed/interpretability/re-runnability -- exactly the white-box decision-support tone judges reward."
section_highlights: "Summary cleanly states the two-algorithm architecture and the headline Superior->Ontario insight up front. Section 1.3 'Overview of Linear Programs' pre-justifies the modeling choice with explicit pros AND cons (linearity limits, infeasibility opacity) -- rare honesty that builds credibility. Sections 2.1-2.3 build the model in layered order: network picture (Fig 3 mermaid graph) -> time-expansion -> full algebraic LP with every constraint annotated in prose. Section 2.5 is the data-grounding showcase (cross-correlation lag matrix Table 1, regression plots Fig 6 with R^2). Section 4.2 + Figs 10-11 turn the abstract model into the central narrative payoff via Q1/Q2/Q3 inflow stress tests. Section 5 sensitivity analysis isolates the most tenuous parameter (omega, river decay) and reports net-error sweep honestly. Dedicated Strengths/Weaknesses (6.1/6.2), a Future Work section, a plain-language Memo to the IJC, and a full Notation appendix (Tables 2-3) round out a textbook MCM structure."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The omega (St. Lawrence flow-decay) parameter is admittedly tenuous -- no dam-status data to fit it -- and sensitivity analysis shows net flow error swings ~920-1010 as omega varies in [0.70,0.825], so a load-bearing parameter is poorly identified
Mechanistic model is brittle to parameter mis-measurement by the authors' own admission; robustness to climate is good but robustness to physical-parameter error is weak

Stakeholder 'ideal' curves are hand-crafted sinusoids with subjectively chosen amplitudes/frequencies and qualitative justifications -- not validated against any stakeholder-utility data; the weighting still requires manual seed weights w_0

Strong simplifications: constant lake surface area, 5-node collapse ignoring Lake St. Clair and auxiliaries, dams open/close fully within an hour with zero downtime, single distributary regression per lake (Detroit R^2 only 0.850)
The microschedule flow model f_t = omega*f_{t-1} + k(1-omega)*x_t (convex-combination smoothing) is acknowledged as physically hard to ground in available data

Some result figures are noisy/auto-extracted and internally inconsistent (e.g., a Q2 Superior plot showing ~18.3 m instead of ~183 m, stray 'nan' endpoints), and the weaknesses section has a telling typo ('model will occasionally determine that an adequate schedule is possible' where 'impossible' is meant) -- the LP-infeasibility / forced-flooding case is real but under-handled
Exogenous inflow reduced to ~20% precipitation share and spline-from-monthly-totals/30, smoothing away exactly the extreme short-duration events (like 2017) that motivate the problem
reusable_patterns: Time-expanded network -> LP: any problem with transport/propagation delays on a flow graph can be linearized by node-replication per timestep (Ford-Fulkerson), turning dynamics into a solvable static LP
Use L1/absolute-deviation objectives (linearized) when you want a fast, interpretable, white-box optimizer and need to defend the modeling choice to non-technical decision-makers
Estimate hidden lags/coupling empirically with time-lagged cross-correlation on provided time series instead of assuming them -- cheap, data-justified, and quotable
Justify a linear regression physically (linearize a known nonlinear law like Bernoulli for small oscillations) to earn the right to use it -- pairs an R^2 with a mechanism

Two-tier multigranular decomposition (coarse long-horizon planner + fine short-horizon executor) joined by a terminal/boundary handoff constraint -- a clean way to satisfy prompts asking about multiple timescales
Receding-horizon / rolling control with a lookahead buffer sized to the system's longest lag, plus error-proportional re-weighting to handle conflicting multi-stakeholder objectives fairly
Robustness = percentile scenario sweep (Q1/Q2/Q3 of the driving input) reported side-by-side; sensitivity = isolate your single most uncertain parameter and report the error sweep honestly
Separate hard safety constraints (caps/floors in the constraint set) from soft preferences (penalties in the objective) so the model never trades away catastrophe avoidance
Pre-empt critique by writing an explicit pros-and-cons paragraph for your core method before using it -- disarms judges and signals maturity
